reasoning: The probability of the WHO declaring a new pandemic by June 30, 2025 is very low. The base rate for global pandemics is approximately one every 10-20 years. Over a remaining window of roughly 4 months from March 2 to June 30, 2025, the baseline probability is less than 2%. The principal current threats are H5N1 (avian influenza) and mpox. Mpox is already designated a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), and since it is not a rapidly spreading respiratory pathogen, it historically has not resulted in a "pandemic" declaration by WHO despite its spread. Regarding H5N1, while the outbreak among US dairy cattle and recent isolated human cases continue to be tracked closely (including a January 2025 fatality), experts currently state that sustained human-to-human transmission is currently lacking and a pandemic is "not inevitable". Furthermore, for a novel pathogen to trigger a pandemic declaration by the WHO, a distinct timeline is typically required: emergence, efficient human-to-human spread, global spread, and WHO deliberation (for COVID-19, this took ~2.5 months). Given the lack of any explosive outbreaks as of March 2, 2025, the window for a new pandemic to reach the "declaration" threshold by June 30 is extremely tight. While the prediction market traded at 0.07 in late February, prediction markets on low-probability tail-risk events typically trade at inflated prices due to longshot bias, capital efficiency, and retail sentiment. A realistic evaluation suggests a probability of ~4%.
